# **Declaration**

The purpose of this Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document is to identify and document the user requirements for Q-ARMOR (Quantum-Aware Mapping & Observation for Risk Remediation). The end deliverable software will comprise of all the requirements documented in the current document and will be operated in the manner specified herein. The source code has been developed based on these requirements and will formally go through code review during the testing process.

**Annexure-A: CERT-IN CBOM Elements**

# **1\. Introduction**

## **1.1 Purpose**

Q-ARMOR (Quantum-Aware Mapping & Observation for Risk Remediation) is a production-quality Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness scanner designed to discover, probe, classify, assess, and remediate the cryptographic posture of internet-facing assets. It evaluates endpoints against the NIST FIPS 203 (ML-KEM), FIPS 204 (ML-DSA), and FIPS 205 (SLH-DSA) post-quantum standards.

The purpose of this Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document is to identify and document the user requirements for Q-ARMOR. This document is prepared with the following objectives:

* To provide the behaviour of the system and its scanning pipeline.

* To provide Process Flow charts and architectural diagrams.

* To document functional and non-functional requirements for PNB's Quantum-Proof Systems Scanner.

* To describe the technology stack, security constraints, and compliance requirements.

## **1.2 Scope**

Q-ARMOR covers the full lifecycle of PQC compliance assessment across the following phases:

| Phase | Capability | Core Module(s) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Phase 1 | Asset Discovery & TLS Protocol Analysis | discoverer.py, prober.py, classifier.py |
| Phase 2 | PQC Assessment & Remediation | assessment.py, remediation.py, nist\_matrix.py |
| Phase 3 | CycloneDX 1.7 CBOM Generation | cbom\_generator.py |
| Phase 4 | 3-Tier Certification Labeling Engine | labeler.py |
| Phase 5 | Compliance-as-Code Attestation & Automation | attestor.py, notifier.py |
| Phase 6 | Tri-Mode Probing & Asset Discovery Foundation | discoverer.py, prober.py, demo\_data.py |
| Phase 7 | PQC Classification \+ Agility Assessment \+ SQLite DB | classifier.py, agility\_assessor.py, database.py |
| Phase 8 | Regression Detection \+ CycloneDX 1.7 CBOM | regression\_detector.py, cbom\_generator.py |
| Phase 9 | PQC Labeling \+ Label Registry \+ FIPS Attestation | labeler.py, label\_registry.py, attestor.py |

**In Scope:**

* TLS handshake analysis via Python ssl and openssl s\_client subprocess.

* PQC classification based on negotiated server parameters only.

* Q-Score quantification (0-100) across 5 dimensions.

* CycloneDX CBOM and CDXA attestation document generation.

* Digital signing of attestations using Ed25519.

* Alert detection and Slack/Teams webhook integration.

* CI/CD build-breaking gate for quantum risk.

* Web-based interactive dashboard and CLI scanner.

* Enterprise-wide console: GUI dashboard displaying status of all scanned public-facing applications including CBOM elements as per Annexure-A (CERT-IN CBOM Elements).

* Issue digital labels: Quantum-Safe, PQC Ready, or Fully Quantum Safe, displayed on dashboard with High/Medium/Low severity ratings.

**Out of Scope:**

* Active exploitation or penetration testing.

* Certificate Authority (CA) operations.

* Quantum key distribution (QKD) hardware.

* Real-time network packet capture.

## **1.3 Intended Audience**

| Audience | Interest |
| :---- | :---- |
| CISOs & Security Architects | Enterprise PQC migration strategy and compliance reporting |
| DevSecOps Engineers | CI/CD integration, CBOM generation, automated alerting |
| Compliance Officers | NIST FIPS compliance attestation documents |
| Network Administrators | TLS configuration auditing and remediation guidance |
| Developers | API integration, extending the scanner |
| Auditors | Verifiable, signed compliance evidence (CDXA) |
| Bank Cybersecurity Teams & IT Administrators (Primary Users) | Scanning, assessment, and dashboard management |
| Compliance Auditors & Risk Managers (Secondary Users) | Report review, risk acceptance, audit evidence |
| PNB / IIT Kanpur Officials | Admin console access for enterprise-wide monitoring |

# **2\. Overall Description**

## **2.1 Product Perspective**

Q-ARMOR operates as a standalone security assessment platform with persistent storage. The system consists of three primary components:

* Backend Server — A FastAPI application providing 45+ RESTful API endpoints with SQLite persistence.

* Web Dashboard — A single-page application (SPA) served by the backend with a dark-themed cybersecurity interface.

* CLI Scanner — A standalone command-line tool for batch scanning with rich terminal output.

**System Architecture Diagram:**

| *![][image1]* |
| :---: |

## **2.2 Product Functions**

| ID | Function | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| F1 | Asset Discovery | DNS resolution, subdomain enumeration via Certificate Transparency logs |
| F2 | TLS Probing | Full TLS handshake, certificate parsing, negotiated group extraction via openssl s\_client |
| F3 | PQC Classification | Q-Score (0-100) with 5-dimension breakdown: TLS version, key exchange, certificate, cipher strength, agility |
| F4 | Status Assignment | 5-tier: Fully Quantum Safe, PQC Transition, Quantum Vulnerable, Critically Vulnerable, Unknown |
| F5 | NIST Assessment | 4-dimension evaluation against FIPS 203/204/205 matrix (TLS, KEX, certificate, symmetric) |
| F6 | Remediation | Priority-tier (P1-P4) actionable recommendations with NIST/RFC references and timelines |
| F7 | CBOM Export | CycloneDX 1.7 Cryptographic Bill of Materials in JSON format |
| F8 | Certification Labels | 3-tier certification: Fully Quantum Safe / PQC Ready / Non-Compliant |
| F9 | CDXA Attestation | Ed25519-signed compliance attestation with NIST FIPS evidence claims and 90-day validity |
| F10 | Alert Detection | HNDL vulnerability, HIGH quantum risk, non-compliance threshold, crypto downgrade alerts |
| F11 | Webhook Notifications | Slack and Microsoft Teams integration via incoming webhooks |
| F12 | CI/CD Gate | Build-breaking exit code when HIGH quantum risk endpoints detected (--ci flag) |
| F13 | Demo Mode | 21 simulated bank assets covering all 5 PQC status categories |
| F14 | Regression Detection | Cross-scan comparison detecting new assets, score regressions, and missed PQC upgrades |
| F15 | Label Registry | Append-only label log with auto-revoke on algorithm regression or certificate expiry |
| F16 | Crypto Agility | 5-indicator scoring: CDN, software currency, ACME CA, protocol flexibility, SAN diversity |
| F17 | SQLite Persistence | WAL-mode SQLite database with 4 tables: scans, asset\_scores, labels, alerts — full CRUD \+ delta comparison |
| F18 | Enterprise Dashboard | GUI console showing High/Medium/Low ratings per scanned public application with CBOM details |

## **2.3 User Classes and Characteristics**

| User Class | Technical Level | Primary Interactions | User Type |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Security Analyst | Expert | CLI scanning, CBOM review, CDXA verification | Primary User |
| CISO / Manager | Intermediate | Web dashboard, summary reports, compliance attestations | Primary User |
| DevOps Engineer | Expert | CI/CD integration, API calls, webhook setup | Primary User |
| Compliance Auditor | Low-Medium | Attestation download, label verification | Secondary User |
| Network Admin | High | Endpoint scanning, remediation execution | Primary User |
| PNB / IIT Kanpur Officials | High | Admin console, enterprise management | Admin User |
| PNB Checker | Intermediate | Review scan results, approve or escalate findings | Checker User |

## **2.4 Operating Environment**

The operating environment for Q-ARMOR is as follows:

| Component | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| Server System | Linux / macOS / Windows (WSL) — any server with Python 3.10+ |
| Operating System | Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+), macOS 12+, or Windows 11 with WSL2 |
| Runtime | Python 3.10+ (developed on Python 3.12.3) |
| Database | SQLite (WAL mode) — primary file: data/scanner.db |
| Platform | FastAPI \+ Uvicorn ASGI server |
| Technology | Python, OpenSSL, CycloneDX 1.7 |
| API | RESTful HTTP/JSON API (45+ endpoints) |
| External Binary | openssl (version 1.1.1+ or 3.x) in system PATH |
| Network | Outbound TCP 443 (or custom ports) to scan targets |
| Browser | Any modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) |
| Memory | Minimum 512 MB RAM for 20-endpoint scans |
| Disk | \~50 MB for application \+ dependencies; SQLite DB grows with scan history |
| Frontend Hosting | Vercel (static hosting, edge delivery) |
| Backend Hosting | Railway (production deployment of FastAPI service) |

## **2.5 Design and Implementation Constraints**

### **1\. Technical Constraints (Deployment)**

* Network Configuration: The application must support both private (intranet) IP addressing and internet-facing HTTPS. Appropriate firewalls and routing rules must be configured for intranet deployment at PNB.

* Hosting Environment: Should be deployable in intranet for internal PNB access as well as internet-facing public applications.

* No PQC False Positives: PQC status is derived exclusively from the ServerHello negotiated group, never from client cipher offers.

* Deterministic TLS Parsing: Uses openssl s\_client subprocess for key exchange group extraction since Python's ssl module does not expose this information.

* UNKNOWN on Failure: If TLS parsing fails or returns insufficient data, the status is marked UNKNOWN, never defaulting to a vulnerability assumption.

### **2\. Security Constraints**

* Access Control: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) — Admin User vs PNB Checker roles.

* Data Encryption: All data transmitted over the internet must use HTTPS (TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred).

* Ed25519 Key Persistence: CDXA signing keys are auto-generated and stored in .keys/ directory.

* SNI Support: All TLS probes include Server Name Indication (SNI) for virtual-hosted environments.

### **3\. Performance Constraints**

* Failover Mechanisms: Ensure redundancy and failover mechanisms are in place for both environments to maintain availability.

* Async probing and batched processing support larger asset sets; SQLite WAL mode provides concurrent read access.

* In-Memory Session Cache: Latest scan results are stored in-memory on the server; Phase 7 persists to SQLite.

### **4\. User Interface Constraints**

* User Experience Consistency: Maintain consistent dark-themed design and navigation elements across dashboard and CLI to minimise confusion.

* Must comply with NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203/204/205).

* Must operate only on public-facing applications without disrupting live banking services.

* Must generate reports in machine-readable formats (JSON, XML, CSV).

* Must not disrupt live banking services during scanning.

## **2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies**

**Assumptions:**

* Standard Browser Support: End users will access the dashboard using an HTML5-compliant browser such as Google Chrome, Firefox, or Edge.

* Target servers are reachable over the network from the scanner host.

* openssl binary (version 1.1.1+ or 3.x) is installed and available in the system PATH.

* TLS-based communication is used in all public-facing applications.

* Assumes internet connectivity for scanning live endpoints.

* The demo dataset represents realistic banking infrastructure configurations.

**Dependencies:**

| Package | Version | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| fastapi | 0.115.6 | REST API framework |
| uvicorn | 0.34.0 | ASGI server |
| pydantic | 2.10.4 | Data validation and serialization |
| cryptography | 44.0.0 | X.509 certificate parsing, Ed25519 signing |
| httpx | 0.28.1 | Async HTTP client |
| dnspython | 2.7.0 | DNS resolution and CT log queries |
| rich | 13.0+ | Rich terminal output (tables, panels, colors) |
| requests | 2.31+ | Webhook notification delivery (Slack/Teams) |
| sqlite3 | (stdlib) | Phase 7 persistent storage (WAL mode) |
| jinja2 | 3.1.5 | Template rendering |

The application depends on NIST PQC algorithms (FIPS 203/204/205) being standardised and available. Any updates to NIST standards may require rule-matrix updates.

# **3\. Specific Requirements**

## **3.1 Functional Requirements**

### **FR-1: Asset Discovery**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-1.1 | The system SHALL resolve domain names to IP addresses using DNS A/AAAA records. |
| FR-1.2 | The system SHALL discover subdomains via Certificate Transparency (CT) logs. |
| FR-1.3 | The system SHALL verify DNS reachability before scanning. |
| FR-1.4 | The system SHALL classify discovered assets by type (web, api, vpn, mail, other). |

### **FR-2: TLS Probing**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-2.1 | The system SHALL perform a full TLS handshake with target endpoints. |
| FR-2.2 | The system SHALL extract the negotiated TLS version, cipher suite, and key exchange group. |
| FR-2.3 | The system SHALL parse X.509 certificates including subject, issuer, validity, signature algorithm, and public key details. |
| FR-2.4 | The system SHALL use openssl s\_client \-brief to extract the authoritative ServerHello negotiated group. |
| FR-2.5 | The system SHALL return a partial fingerprint on failure instead of raising an exception. |

### **FR-3: PQC Classification**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-3.1 | The system SHALL compute a Q-Score (0-100) across 5 dimensions: TLS (20) \+ KEX (30) \+ Cert (20) \+ Cipher (15) \+ Agility (15). |
| FR-3.2 | The system SHALL assign one of 5 PQC statuses: FULLY\_QUANTUM\_SAFE, PQC\_TRANSITION, QUANTUM\_VULNERABLE, CRITICALLY\_VULNERABLE, UNKNOWN. |
| FR-3.3 | The system SHALL never infer PQC readiness from client cipher offers. |
| FR-3.4 | The system SHALL mark scan failures as UNKNOWN status. |

### **FR-4: Assessment & Remediation**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-4.1 | The system SHALL evaluate endpoints across 4 cryptographic dimensions against the NIST PQC validation matrix. |
| FR-4.2 | The system SHALL calculate overall quantum risk as HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW. |
| FR-4.3 | The system SHALL detect Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later (HNDL) vulnerability based on key exchange status. |
| FR-4.4 | The system SHALL generate prioritised (P1-P4) remediation actions with concrete steps, timelines, and NIST/RFC references. |

### **FR-5: CBOM Generation**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-5.1 | The system SHALL generate CycloneDX 1.6/1.7 compliant CBOM documents in JSON format. |
| FR-5.2 | Each CBOM component SHALL include cryptographic properties (protocol, cipher suites, key exchange, algorithms) conforming to Annexure-A CERT-IN CBOM Elements. |
| FR-5.3 | The CBOM SHALL be downloadable as a JSON file. |

### **FR-6: Certification Labeling**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-6.1 | The system SHALL assign one of 3 certification tiers: Fully Quantum Safe (Tier 1), PQC Ready (Tier 2), Non-Compliant (Tier 3). |
| FR-6.2 | Tier 1 (Fully Quantum Safe) SHALL require: TLS 1.3 \+ pure PQC KEM \+ PQC certificate chain. |
| FR-6.3 | Tier 2 (PQC Ready) SHALL require: TLS 1.3 \+ hybrid key exchange (classical \+ PQC). |
| FR-6.4 | Tier 3 (Non-Compliant) SHALL capture all endpoints that do not meet Tier 1 or Tier 2\. |

### **FR-7: Compliance Attestation**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-7.1 | The system SHALL generate CycloneDX Attestation (CDXA) documents mapping NIST FIPS 203/204/205 compliance claims. |
| FR-7.2 | Each attestation SHALL be digitally signed using Ed25519. |
| FR-7.3 | Attestation signatures SHALL be independently verifiable. |
| FR-7.4 | Attestations SHALL have a 90-day validity period. |

### **FR-8: Alerting & Automation**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-8.1 | The system SHALL detect HNDL vulnerability, HIGH quantum risk endpoints, non-compliance threshold exceedance, and cryptographic downgrades. |
| FR-8.2 | The system SHALL send alert notifications to Slack via Incoming Webhooks. |
| FR-8.3 | The system SHALL send alert notifications to Microsoft Teams via Adaptive Cards. |
| FR-8.4 | The CI/CD mode SHALL exit with code 1 if any endpoint has HIGH quantum risk. |

### **FR-9: Regression Detection & Historical Tracking**

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| FR-9.1 | The system SHALL produce best-case (Probe A), typical (Probe B), and worst-case (Probe C) Q-Scores per asset using Tri-Mode Probing. |
| FR-9.2 | The system SHALL assess crypto-agility via 5 indicators: CDN, software currency, ACME CA, protocol flexibility, SAN diversity. |
| FR-9.3 | The system SHALL persist scan results, asset scores, labels, and alerts to a SQLite database in WAL mode. |
| FR-9.4 | The system SHALL support delta comparison between any two historical scans. |
| FR-9.5 | The system SHALL detect score regressions of 5 or more points across scans and flag missed PQC upgrades. |
| FR-9.6 | The system SHALL maintain backward compatibility with the legacy 4-dimension classifier. |

## **3.2 External Interface Requirements**

### **3.2.1 User Interfaces**

Web Dashboard (SPA):

* Dark-themed responsive interface served at http://localhost:8000/ and http://localhost:8000/dashboard.

* Tab-based navigation: Overview, PQC Assessment, Remediation Plan, NIST Matrix.

* Landing page at /static/landing.html with cybersecurity grid animated background.

* Interactive demo scan button for instant results with 21 simulated bank assets.

* CBOM and CDXA export functionality (JSON download).

* Real-time rendering of certification labels, attestation status, and security alerts.

* Enterprise dashboard showing High/Medium/Low rating per scanned public application with CBOM CERT-IN elements as per Annexure-A.

**Dashboard Wireframe:**

| *![][image2]* |
| :---: |

CLI Scanner:

* Rich terminal output with coloured tables, panels, and formatted text.

* Supports \--format options: table, json, cbom, assess.

* \--attest flag for CDXA generation; \--ci flag for build-breaking CI/CD gate; \--discover flag for subdomain enumeration.

### **3.2.2 Hardware Interfaces**

Q-ARMOR does not interface directly with hardware. It communicates over standard TCP/TLS network connections to scan target servers. The OpenSSL binary is invoked as a subprocess for TLS negotiation analysis. IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack address resolution is supported via socket.getaddrinfo().

### **3.2.3 Software / Communication Interfaces**

| Interface | Protocol | Format | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Target Servers | TLS 1.0-1.3 | TCP | TLS handshake probing via Python ssl and openssl |
| DNS Servers | DNS | UDP/TCP | Domain resolution and subdomain discovery |
| CT Log Servers | HTTPS | JSON | Certificate Transparency log queries |
| Slack | HTTPS POST | JSON (Block Kit) | Alert notifications via Incoming Webhooks |
| Microsoft Teams | HTTPS POST | JSON (Adaptive Cards) | Alert notifications via connector webhooks |
| Web Browser | HTTP/REST | JSON / HTML | Dashboard UI and API consumption |
| CI/CD Pipeline | Process Exit Code | Integer (0 or 1\) | Build gate signal |

## **3.3 System Features**

### **SF-1: Demo Scan Mode**

* Pre-loaded dataset of 21 simulated bank assets covering all 5 PQC status categories.

* Distribution: 2 Fully Quantum Safe, 4 PQC Transition, 11 Quantum Vulnerable, 3 Critically Vulnerable, 1 Unknown.

* Average Q-Score: 51.6. Instant results without network access.

### **SF-2: Real Domain Scanning**

* Live TLS handshake analysis against real endpoints.

* Parallel async scanning with configurable concurrency (cap: 20 assets).

* DNS discovery \+ CT log enumeration before scanning.

### **SF-3: Tri-Mode Probing**

* Probe A (PQC-Capable): Simulates a modern PQC client requesting ML-KEM-768 or X25519MLKEM768.

* Probe B (Classical TLS 1.3): Simulates a standard modern client without PQC capabilities (X25519).

* Probe C (TLS 1.2 Downgrade): Forces maximum protocol version TLS 1.2 to check for downgrade attack exposure.

**Tri-Mode Probe Flow Diagram:**

| *![][image3]* |
| :---: |

### **SF-4: NIST Validation Matrix**

* Reference database of quantum-vulnerable, quantum-safe, and hybrid algorithms.

* Lookup API for algorithm classification by quantum status.

### **SF-5: Phase 7 — 5-Dimension Q-Score Classification**

| Dimension | Max Score | Scoring Details |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TLS Version | 20 | TLSv1.3 \= 20, TLSv1.2 \= 10, \<= 1.1 \= 2 |
| Key Exchange (KEX) | 30 | ML-KEM \= 30, Hybrid \= 22, ECDHE \= 12 |
| Certificate | 20 | ML-DSA / SLH-DSA \= 20, ECDSA-384 \= 12 |
| Cipher Strength | 15 | 256-bit \= 15, 128-bit \= 10 |
| Agility Score | 15 | 5 indicators x 3 pts each |
| TOTAL | 100 | Best / Typical / Worst per asset |

### **SF-6: Signed Attestation Workflow**

* Automatic Ed25519 keypair generation and persistence in .keys/ directory.

* SHA-256 content hashing \+ Ed25519 digital signature.

* CDXA v2 with per-standard FIPS 203/204/205 compliance claims and 90-day validity window.

* Verification endpoint for independent signature validation.

**Phase 8+9 Pipeline Flow Diagram:**

| *![][image4]* |
| :---: |

## **3.4 Non-Functional Requirements**

### **3.4.1 Performance Requirements**

| Metric | Target |
| :---- | :---- |
| Demo scan response time | \< 500ms |
| Single endpoint TLS probe | \< 10 seconds (including openssl subprocess) |
| Batch scan (20 endpoints) | \< 30 seconds (parallel async) |
| CBOM generation | \< 200ms for 20 endpoints |
| CDXA attestation generation | \< 500ms (includes Ed25519 signing) |
| Dashboard rendering | \< 1 second after API response |
| API response (cached scan) | \< 100ms |

### **3.4.2 Software Quality Attributes**

| Attribute | Implementation |
| :---- | :---- |
| Reliability | Graceful degradation on scan failure (UNKNOWN status); partial fingerprints returned instead of exceptions. |
| Maintainability | Modular architecture with 14 dedicated scanner modules; clear separation of concerns across 9 phases. |
| Security | Ed25519 digital signatures; no PQC false positives; CORS-configured API; no credential storage. |
| Extensibility | New scanner modules can be added by implementing the module pattern; API endpoints are additive. |
| Usability | Intuitive dark-themed dashboard; one-click demo scan; downloadable reports in JSON/CSV. |
| Testability | 21 automated tests covering all classification paths, edge cases, and CBOM format validation. |
| Portability | Cross-platform Python with standard library and widely available dependencies. |
| Documentation | All public API functions have comprehensive docstrings with parameter/return type annotations. |

### **3.4.3 Other Non-Functional Requirements**

| Category | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| Compliance | Output documents conform to CycloneDX 1.6/1.7 specification. |
| Standards | Classification is based on NIST FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205, RFC 8446, RFC 8996, CNSA 2.0. |
| Logging | Structured logging via Python logging module with module-specific loggers (qarmor.prober, qarmor.classifier, etc.). |
| Error Handling | HTTP exceptions with descriptive messages; CLI exits with code 1 on total scan failure or CI gate breach. |
| Concurrency | Async/await pattern for parallel TLS probing; ASGI server with hot-reload for development. |
| Report Formats | Must generate reports in machine-readable formats: JSON, XML, CSV. |

# **4\. Technological Requirements**

## **4.1 Technologies Used in Development of the Web Application**

| Layer | Technology | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Frontend | HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript (ES6+) | Dashboard rendering, charts, UI interaction, API integration |
| Frontend Hosting | Vercel | Static hosting, edge delivery, API rewrite routing to backend |
| Backend API | FastAPI, Uvicorn | REST endpoints, JSON responses, async request handling |
| Programming Language | Python 3.12.3 | Core application logic, scanning pipeline, API runtime |
| Crypto / Parsing | OpenSSL, cryptography (Python) | TLS negotiation analysis, certificate parsing, Ed25519 signing |
| Data / Persistence | SQLite WAL, JSON, CycloneDX 1.7 | Scan history, asset score persistence, CBOM generation |
| Testing / Tooling | pytest, rich | Automated validation and CLI output formatting |
| Backend Hosting | Railway | Production deployment of the FastAPI service |
| Standards | NIST FIPS 203/204/205, CycloneDX 1.7, RFC 8446 | PQC classification, CBOM format, TLS standard compliance |

## **4.2 I.D.E. (Integrated Development Environment)**

The project is developed and maintained primarily in Visual Studio Code with Python tooling for linting, debugging, terminal execution, and repository management.

Recommended VS Code extensions and tooling:

* Python extension for interpreter management, debugging, and test discovery.

* Pylance for language intelligence and type analysis.

* Jupyter support when interactive experimentation is needed.

* Git integration for source control and deployment workflow.

Any modern Python-capable IDE can be used, but the repository and current workflow are structured around VS Code.

## **4.3 Database Management Software**

| Property | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Database Engine | SQLite |
| Storage Mode | WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) |
| Primary File | data/scanner.db |
| Tables | scans, asset\_scores, labels, alerts |
| Usage | Scan history, asset scores, certification labels, alerts |
| Why Chosen | Lightweight, serverless, zero-admin, suitable for single-service deployment |

![][image6]  
![][image7]

# **5\. Diagrams**

![][image8]

# **![][image9]**

# **6\. Security Requirements**

The following security requirements SHALL be satisfied by the Q-ARMOR system at a minimum:

| ID | Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| SR-1 | Compatibility of the proposed system with current IT set-up: Q-ARMOR operates as a passive scanner over existing TLS infrastructure. Existing systems will not be affected by the scanning process. |
| SR-2 | Audit Trails for all important events capturing details like user ID, time and date, event etc.: All scan results, certification label events, and attestation generations are persisted to the SQLite database with timestamps. All API responses are logged. |
| SR-3 | Control Access based on segregation of duty / need-to-know: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is enforced — only Admin users may initiate scheduled scans or revoke labels; PNB Checker users may review but not modify. |
| SR-4 | Recoverability of Application in case of Failure: Application state is recoverable from SQLite WAL database. In the event of server failure, the database file persists and can be restored from the Disaster Recovery (DR) environment. |
| SR-5 | Compliance with legal, statutory, and contractual obligations: The system complies with NIST FIPS 203/204/205 post-quantum cryptography standards, CycloneDX 1.7 SBOM/CBOM specification, RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), and RFC 8996 (TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecation). |
| SR-6 | Security vulnerabilities when connecting with other systems: Integration points (Slack/Teams webhooks, CT log servers, DNS) use HTTPS. Security vulnerabilities introduced via these integrations will be identified during security audit. |
| SR-7 | Operating environment security: TLS 1.2 minimum for all HTTPS communication; TLS 1.3 preferred. The system shall set baseline HTTP security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy. |
| SR-8 | Digital Signature Integrity: All attestation documents shall be digitally signed using Ed25519. The system shall support independent verification of these signatures. |
| SR-9 | No False Positives on PQC Status: The system shall derive PQC posture exclusively from negotiated server parameters, never from client capability assumptions. |
| SR-10 | Label Registry Integrity: The label registry shall be append-only; revoke operations are recorded rather than silently overwritten, ensuring full traceability. |
| SR-11 | No Credential Storage in Frontend: The system shall not store user credentials or API secrets in the dashboard frontend. |
| SR-12 | Cost of providing security over system lifecycle: Includes OpenSSL binary updates, Python dependency updates, NIST standard conformance reviews, and staff training for PNB security teams. |

# **Annexure-A: CERT-IN CBOM Elements**

Table 9: Minimum Elements pertaining to Cryptographic Asset — as required by the enterprise dashboard display (per Section 1.2 Scope).

| Cryptographic Asset Type | Element | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Algorithms | Name | The name of the cryptographic algorithm or asset. E.g., AES-128-GCM refers to the AES algorithm with a 128-bit key in Galois/Counter Mode (GCM). |
| Algorithms | Asset Type | Specifies the type of cryptographic asset. For algorithms, the asset type is algorithm. |
| Algorithms | Primitive | Describes the cryptographic primitive. E.g., for SHA512withRSA, the primitive is signature. |
| Algorithms | Mode | The operational mode used by the algorithm. E.g., gcm refers to Galois/Counter Mode used with AES encryption. |
| Algorithms | Crypto Functions | The cryptographic functions supported. E.g., for AES-128-GCM: key generation, encryption, decryption, and authentication tag generation. |
| Algorithms | Classical Security Level | Represents the strength of the cryptographic asset in terms of resistance to classical (non-quantum) attacks. E.g., 128 bits for AES-128. |
| Algorithms | OID | The Object Identifier (OID) globally identifying the algorithm. E.g., 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.1.6 for AES-128-GCM. |
| Algorithms | List | Lists the cryptographic algorithms employed, allowing assessment of security capabilities in the context of post-quantum encryption standards. |
| Keys | Name | The name/unique identifier for the key used in cryptographic operations. |
| Keys | Asset Type | Defines the type of cryptographic asset. For keys, the asset type is typically key. |
| Keys | id | A unique identifier for the key, such as a key ID or reference number. |
| Keys | state | The state of the key: active, revoked, or expired. |
| Keys | size | The size of the key in bits. E.g., a 128-bit or 2048-bit RSA key. |
| Keys | Creation Date | The date when the key was created. |
| Keys | Activation Date | The date when the key became operational or was first used. |
| Protocols | Name | The name of the cryptographic protocol: TLS, IPsec, or SSH. |
| Protocols | Asset Type | Defines the type of cryptographic asset. For protocols, the asset type is protocol. |
| Protocols | Version | The version of the protocol used, such as TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3. |
| Protocols | Cipher Suites | The set of cryptographic algorithms and parameters supported by the protocol for tasks like encryption, key exchange, and integrity checking. |
| Protocols | OID | The Object Identifier associated with the protocol, identifying its unique specifications. |
| Certificates | Name | The name of the certificate, typically referring to its subject or the entity it represents (e.g., a website). |
| Certificates | Asset Type | Defines the type. For certificates, the asset type is certificate. |
| Certificates | Subject Name | The Distinguished Name (DN) of the entity the certificate represents: organisation, domain name. |
| Certificates | Issuer Name | The Certificate Authority (CA) that issued and signed the certificate — the DN of the CA. |
| Certificates | Not Valid Before | The date and time from which the certificate is valid. |
| Certificates | Not Valid After | The expiration date and time of the certificate. |
| Certificates | Signature Algorithm Reference | The cryptographic algorithm used to sign the certificate, with reference to its OID. |
| Certificates | Subject Public Key Reference | Points to the public key used by the subject, providing a reference to the key details and algorithm. |
| Certificates | Certificate Format | Specifies the format of the certificate. E.g., X.509, which is the most widely used format. |
| Certificates | Certificate Extension | The file extension associated with the certificate. Commonly .crt for X.509 format certificates. |
